coredata.dc:title: Pattern-based discrimination and visualization of metal ions through RGB coding of multicolor-emitting carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Karami, Sara
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Barati, Ali
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the single para-Phenylenediamine (PPD) source was used to synthesis three orange-, green-, and blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) under different synthesis conditions. Due to the diverse fluorescence response of the synthesized CDs to heavy metal ions such as Au3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, a three-channel sensor array system for pattern-based discrimination and visualization of these metal ions was developed. The recorded fluorescence data was successfully classified into separate clusters without any overlapping through typical statistical methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA). We also introduced a new method of discrimination and determination by coding the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs into integer values from 0 to 255 and converting these values into a secondary color using the RGB additive mixing method to provide a single, unique, and visual fingerprint-like color for each metal ion. The colors obtained not only identified the selected metal ions and their binary and ternary mixed samples, but also provided a concentration-dependent trend of each specific color for metal ion quantification. Furthermore, the practicability of the constructed sensor array and the proposed RGB coding method was validated by the successful identification and quantification of unknown metal ions in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensor array
RGB
Metal ions
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to metal ions
Sensor array construction for discrimination and visualization of metal ions
Practicability of the sensor array in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images (a), XPS survey spectra and the deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of C1 s and N1 s regions (b), and UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (c) of b-, g-, and o-CDs.

Left side: Fluorescence spectra of b-, g-, and o-CDs to the selected metal ions (0.2 −70 μM) in PBS, at pH 6.0 (excited at 320, 420, and 520 nm, respectively). Right side: Plots of fluorescence maximum intensities of b-, g-, and o-CDs versus metal ion concentrations.

Response pattern graph of three CDs sensor array against the seven selected metal ions at the concentrations of 1 μM (A) and 10 μM (B). C). The related three dimensional score plots obtained from the PCA analyses of the response patterns (35 samples, five replicates of each ion) (D), and the related hierarchical cluster analyses (E and F). (The excitation wavelength for b-, g-, and o-CDs were 320, 420, and 520 nm, respectively).

A) The three dimensional PCA plot of the fluorescence response of the mixed CDs solution sensor array to selected metal ions at the concentration range of 0.2–70 μM. B) The related visual fingerprint-like patterns of the selected metal ions at different concentrations obtained through RGB coding method.

A and B) The visual fingerprint-like patterns of the singular, binary, and ternary mixed samples of the presented metal ions obtained through RGB coding method on the fluorescence responses of the mixed CDs solution sensor array. C and D) The related three dimensional PCA plots of the fluorescence responses.

A) The fingerprint-like color obtained for the unknown concentrations (2 and 8 µM) of the presented singular and binary mixed metal ion samples in commercial mineral water on the fingerprint-like color obtained from the standard samples (0.2–70 μM). B) The related three-dimensional PCA plot of the fluorescence response (unknown samples are shown as stars).
A select of the most important fluorescence sensor arrays reported for the discrimination and determination of metal ions.

row.entry: Interactions between the surface groups of QDs (-OH, -NH2, and –COOH) and metal ions
Ni2+, Co2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+
The soft acid-soft base interaction between metal ions and S atoms
Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Au3+, Fe2+
The electrostatic interaction, adsorbed molecular status, complexation, and cation-π correlation
The various surface states of CDs led to diverse quenching responses to different ions
Hg2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Eu3+, Pb2+, Fe3+
Non-specific collective recognition reactions between metal ions and CDs
Ag+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+
The interactions between different N, C, O- contents on the CDs surface and metal ions
Ni2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Hg2+
Different acceptor ligands on the surface of QDs
Target metal ions were selectively separated by an on-chip SPE b and then automatically transferred to the DNA aptamer-linked GQD
The interaction between the terminal receptor (-NH2), or (-SH) of CQDs and ions

Specific interactions between ion species and heteroatoms on GQDs and aggregation induced fluorescence quenching between GQDs
(Use of different masking agents such as AA d , Cys e and EDTA f )
The different affinity of CDs to metal ions due to varied element content of CDs
Interaction between EDTA with metal ions through its carboxyl and amino groups. This interaction may affect the interaction of Tb3+ with EDTA, influencing the responses of EDTA-Tb3+.
Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Cr6+

entry.bold: Carbon dots and a porphyrin derivative (TMPyP)

row.entry: Interaction of metal ions with the surface groups of the free CDs, TMPyP and CDs–TMPyP complex.
Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+
Interaction between the various surface groups of CDs and metal ion
Au3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ Pb2+, Fe3+

head.ce:title: Pattern-based discrimination and visualization of metal ions through RGB coding of multicolor-emitting carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the single para-Phenylenediamine (PPD) source was used to synthesis three orange-, green-, and blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) under different synthesis conditions. Due to the diverse fluorescence response of the synthesized CDs to heavy metal ions such as Au3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, a three-channel sensor array system for pattern-based discrimination and visualization of these metal ions was developed. The recorded fluorescence data was successfully classified into separate clusters without any overlapping through typical statistical methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA). We also introduced a new method of discrimination and determination by coding the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs into integer values from 0 to 255 and converting these values into a secondary color using the RGB additive mixing method to provide a single, unique, and visual fingerprint-like color for each metal ion. The colors obtained not only identified the selected metal ions and their binary and ternary mixed samples, but also provided a concentration-dependent trend of each specific color for metal ion quantification. Furthermore, the practicability of the constructed sensor array and the proposed RGB coding method was validated by the successful identification and quantification of unknown metal ions in real water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the widespread presence of metal ions in biological, chemical, and environmental systems, the accurate measurement of these ions has always been considered as an important research topic in analytical chemistry [1,2]. Atomic absorption spectroscopy [3], and inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [4] are the most commonly used techniques for the quantification of metal ions. However, these laboratory techniques require very expensive equipment and highly trained analysts, and they are not suitable for on-site analysis.

Nowadays, fluorescence methods with advantages such as high sensitivity, a simple detection process, and inexpensive equipment are being studied and applied as complementary methods to overcome these limitations [5–7]. Most fluorescence methods rely on the traditional “lock-key” sensing mode, where each selective fluorescent probe responds to a specific target. Most of these probes rarely fulfil all the requirements to be considered as true selective probes and they mostly respond to multiple targets but with different sensitivities. However, as a valuable advantage, non-selective fluorescent probes can be used to develop sensor array systems for identification and simultaneous determination of multi-target ions [8–10]. In an array system, each sensor in the array responds differently to a specific target, so the combined response of the sensors provides a specific fingerprint for each target. Sensor array systems are usually coupled with chemometric methods such as principle component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) to increase the efficiency of this method for discrimination and determination different targets such as metal ions,[11–13] drugs,[14] thiols,[15] proteins,[16] and small molecules.[17].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of fluorescent carbon nanostructures with interesting features such as inexpensive and convenient production, ease of surface functionalization, high water solubility, and low toxicity have found notable applications in various fields of chemistry during the last decade [18]. Interestingly, CDs exhibit different optical properties and sensing responses depending on their surface functional groups which are mostly controlled by the initial material sources and reaction conditions used for their production [19,20]. These features have made CDs interesting candidates to construct sensor array systems [21,22].

Herein, we have synthesized three blue-, green-, and orange-emitting CDs derived from para-phenylenediamine (PPD) under different reaction conditions with different fluorescence responses to seven metal ions. The mixed solution of these three CDs was used as a sensor array to discriminate seven metal ions including Au3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cd2+. Interestingly, by normalizing the fluorescence intensity of the CDs and mixing them through the RGB coding method, a single color as a unique fingerprint for each metal ion was provided. The method was succeeded in determining different concentrations of metal ions and also in analyzing their binary and ternary mixtures.

PPD and urea were purchased from Sigma- Aldrich Chemicals Co. NaOH, HCl, H2SO4, H3PO4, H2O2, and all the metal salts, including AgNO3, HAuCl4·3 H2O, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl3, Cu(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, CdSO4, Pb(NO3)2, and other nitrate salts of metal ions were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and were used without further purification. Deionized water was used to prepare of solutions.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp source. The excitation and emission slits were set at 5 nm, and all measurements were made in a 1 cm quartz cell. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded using an Agilent 8453 diode array spectrophotometer, and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker infrared spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using a Zeiss-EM10C transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 80 kV (K.N.Toosi University of Technology, Tehran, Iran), and XPS spectra were recorded by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, 8025-BesTec twin anode XR3E2 X-ray source system). MATLAB 2017 was used for all chemometric analyses.

There have been numerous reports on the use of phenylenediamine isomers in the synthesis of CDs [23–29]. Here, we synthesized blue-, green-, and orange-emitting CDs using a single isomer of PPD by the following procedures:
1)
Blue-emitting CDs (b-CDs): Briefly, 0.03 g PPD was dissolved in 5 mL of concentrated H2SO4 and 3 mL H2O, then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 ℃ for 4 h.
2)
Green-emitting CDs (g-CDs): Briefly, 0.05 g PPD was dissolved in 5 mL H2O2 (30%). This step produced a dark solution with no fluorescence. This solution was then stirred at room temperature for 2.5 h to prepare green-emitting CDs.
3)
Orange-emitting CDs (o-CDs): Briefly, 0.05 g PPD and 0.02 g urea were dissolved in 5 mL of concentrated H3PO4, then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 4 h.

In all cases, the resulting solutions were first centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min and passed through a 0.2 µm membrane filter to remove the large particles, and then dialyzed against deionized water with a membrane bag (Mw = 3000 Da) for 18 h to remove the additional precursors. The final solutions were freeze-dried and their stock solutions were stored in the refrigerator for further use.

The mixed CD solution with the desired concentrations of b-, g-, and o-CDs (2, 3, and 6 µg/mL, respectively, in 10 mL of 0.1 M PBS) was prepared to construct the sensing array system. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 6.0 using high concentration NaOH and HCl solutions. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the mixed solution were recorded after the addition of each metal ion (0.2–70 µM) under three excitation wavelengths 320, 420, and 520 nm, respectively. The recorded spectra were then analyzed using PCA, HCA, and RGB coding method. For binary and triple metal ion samples, each of the metal ions at the determined concentration was added to the mixed CD solution, and similar data analysis procedures were applied to their recorded fluorescence spectra.

For the identification and quantification of metal ions in real samples, commercial mineral water was used without any purification. To 1.0 mL of water samples 1.0 mL of PBS at pH 6.0 and desired volumes of the stock solutions of the b-, g-, and o-CDs (as their final concentrations were 2, 3, and 6 µg/mL, respectively) were added. Individual or binary mixed solutions of metal ions at concentrations of 2 and 8 µM were then spiked and the CDs fluorescence spectra were recorded. The average of five replicates of the fluorescence spectra was used for all analyses. Metal ions at the same concentrations were added to the binary and ternary mixed samples.

Based on the TEM images shown in Fig. 1a, the three b-, g-, and o-CDs were mono-dispersed with relatively narrow size distributions and average diameters of almost 7 nm, 5 nm, and 4 nm, respectively. The XRD patterns of the b- and o-CDs showed sharp peaks at 20–26 degrees corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphitic crystal structure, and for the g-CDs a broad peak was observed in this region, indicating their amorphous structure (Fig. S1). The FTIR spectra of the CDs showed (Fig. S2) similar O−H/N − H stretching vibrations at 3200–3510 cm−1,[30] the C-N/ C-O vibrations at about 1100 cm−1, and the N = N/CC vibrations at 1620–1640 cm−1 [30,31]. The apparent stretching vibration of COOH bonds at 1750 cm−1 indicated the high degree of oxidation of g-CDs surface [32,33]. In addition, peaks related to P-N at about 933 cm−1, and P = O or P–O groups at 1060 cm−1 were detected for o-CDs [34,35].

The XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. 1b) showed common peaks of C1 s, N1 s, and O1 s at around 285, 400, and 531 eV, respectively. In addition, P2p (134.2 eV) and P2 s (191 eV) peaks and S2p (170 eV) S2 (234 eV) peaks were also found in the XPS spectra of b-CDs and o-CDs, respectively. Deconvolution of C1 s peaks revealed five peaks corresponding to CC (284.3 eV), C-C/ C-N (285.3 eV), C-O (286.25 eV), CO/CN (287.1 eV), and COOH (288.6 eV) groups [36,37]. From the deconvolution of N1 s peaks of CDs, five different peaks were obtained at around 398.4, 399.4, 400.5, 401.7, and 405.9 eV respectively related to pyridinic, amino, pyrrolic, graphitic, and N-O/N-O2 forms of nitrogen [31]. These results show the highest graphitic nitrogen content in o-CDs [38]. Moreover, the higher content of the oxidized form of the carbon (COOH) and nitrogen (N-O/N-O2) elements in the C1 s and N1 s spectra of g-CD indicates the high degree of the surface oxidation of g-CD, which is certainly due to H2O2 that is a strong oxidant [39].

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of the CDs (Fig. 1c) showed the highest absorption bands of b-, g-, and o-CDs at 306, 380, and 490 nm respectively, and few typical bands of CDs at 200–300 nm. Here, the absorption bands in the high energy region (200–300 nm) are mainly related to the π-π * transitions of CC structures in the aromatic rings [24]. The absorption bands in the low energy region (higher than 300 nm) are attributed to the n-π * transition of different surface states of CDs provided by CN and CO groups [23,31]. The emission spectra of CDs are also shown in Fig. 1c. As can be seen, the three CDs show almost excitation-wavelength-independent emission behavior with maximum emissions at 436, 536, and 590 nm for b-, g-, and o-CDs, respectively. These emission maxima are correlated with the absorption bands of CDs, where the CD with the highest absorption band shows emission maxima at the longest wavelength and vice versa. This is in good agreement with our recent report that doping of chromophores in CDs shifts their emission spectra towards longer wavelengths [40]. The quantum yields (QY) of the b-, g-, and o-CDs were measured to be 38%, 29%, and 13%, respectively, which decreased to 29%, 25%, and 15% in the mixed solution. Since the QY of the o-CDs without the urea was only about 6%, urea was used in the synthesis process to compensate for the low QY value of this CD compared to two others in the mixed solution.

Moreover, it has previously been shown that the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs shift the emission of CDs to the longer wavelengths through the surface traps provided on the surface [41]. This is why g-CDs emit at longer wavelengths than the b-CDs. About the o-CDs, these CDs exhibit a high graphitic nitrogen content that is verified as a main factor in the long-wavelength emission of CDs [38]. It has already been shown that P, N, and S- doping in CDs can modify their fluorescence QY and emission properties [42–44]. For example, phosphorus doping in CDs has been shown to increase their fluorescence intensity and red-shift their emission maximum due to the morphological alteration in P-doped CDs compared to undoped CDs [45]. It is reported that phosphorus doping leads to the formation of an electron-rich system in P-doped CDs, more isolated sp2 carbon clusters, defect sites in CDs [46–48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in detail in the characterization section, these multicolor CDs exhibited different types of surface groups and doped elements (N, S, and P) which can provide different tendencies and fluorescence responses against different metal ions. Therefore, the fluorescence response of the CDs against various metal ions including Li+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Au3+, Hg2+, and Pb2+ was investigated (Fig. S3). From the results, Au3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ ions were found to quench at least one of the three CDs in the mixed solution. On the other hand, each CD was quenched by more than one metal ion, but with different intensities. Therefore, the fluorescence responses of these CDs in an array system can be used to discriminate between metal ions.

The fluorescence response of a mixed solution of CDs to metal ions at the three excitation wavelengths 320, 420, and 520 nm for b-, g-, and o-CDs, respectively, was tested in the pH range 5.0 - 9.0. The most fluorescence responses were observed at pH 6.0, together with the highest discrimination ability against metal ions. It seems that at lower pH values the protonation of the binding sites of the CDs and at higher pH values the formation of metal hydroxides reduces the interaction of CDs with metal ions [22]. The fluorescence response of three CDs in the mixed solution under the addition of different concentrations of seven metal ions is shown in Fig. 2. As can be seen, three CDs showed different fluorescence responses to the metal ions. From the results, o CDs were the most sensitive probe for metal ions, which was quenched by Au3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ ions. g-CDs showed significant quenching in the presence of Cd2+ and lesser quenching by Au3+, Ag+, and Hg2+ ions, and b-CDs were remarkably quenched by Au3+.

The origin of fluorescence in CDs has been attributed to electron-hole recombination in carbon-core states, fluorophore molecular states, and/or aromatic domain states [40]. The surface functional groups of CDs can also provide surface states that can regulate the emission from these states. Therefore, modification of the CDs’ surface or chemical interaction of their surface functional groups with a sensing element, such as metal ions, affects their emission. Due to the presence of different functional groups on the surface of the synthesized CDs, each metal ion presents a different binding ability to the CDs, and therefore, quenches their fluorescence to a certain extent through mechanisms such as restriction of the electron-hole recombination in CDs, electron or energy transfer process between CDs and metal ions, CDs aggregation, and else.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor array construction for discrimination and visualization of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The maximum fluorescence intensities of three CDs for metal ions are shown in Fig. 3A and B for two concentrations of 1 and 10 µM. As can be seen, these triple-channel fluorescence responses provide unique fingerprint-like patterns for each metal ion. Encouraged by these results, a fluorescence sensor array system composed of these three CDs was developed to discriminate the seven selected metal ions. In this way, the obtained two-dimensional response pattern data (105 data points, 3 CD channels × 35 metal ions (5 replicates for 7 metal ions)) provided by the sensor array and metal ions were analyzed using PCA, which is a statistical method for transforming observed data into a novel set of linearly uncorrelated principal components (PCs) or factors [49,50].

As shown in Fig. 3C and D, the selected metal ions are well classified into separate clusters without any overlapping in the three-dimensional PCA plots. The leave-one-out cross-validation method was applied to these data and a classification accuracy of 100% was obtained. Moreover, two-dimensional PCA plots using each double set of the three factors also provided separated clusters, but in some cases with lower resolution (Fig. S4). The discriminative ability of the designed sensor array was further verified by HCA, which can distinguish the data points according to their spatial Euclidean distances [51–53]. Similar to the PCA plots, the HCA analysis results of both data sets showed that all metal ions were correctly assigned to their respective groups with no misclassification, Fig. 3E and F.

In addition, to show the excellent discriminative ability and high resolution of the sensor array in the quantification analysis of metal ions in different concentrations, the response patterns obtained in the presence of metal ions at various concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 70 μM were also analyzed using PCA. From Fig. 4A, different concentrations of metal ions produced concentration-dependent curves that originated from the same point (the blank point), but each one expanded along a separate path in the resulted three-dimensional PCA plot.

Due to the large differences in the excitation wavelengths of the three CDs, it was not possible to observe the fluorescence color of the mixed solution of CDs under a single excitation wavelength. Therefore, to visualize the obtained response patterns the RGB pattern visualization method was sued, which provides a single color from the fluorescence signals of the three CDs in the sensor array as a visual fingerprint-like output for each metal ion. In this way, the maximum fluorescence intensities of the o-, g-, and b-CDs were first normalized to the values between 0 and 255 and then utilized as the red, green, and blue primary colors to generate a secondary color by the additive mixing method. The resulting visual fingerprint-like patterns for different concentrations of the selected metal ions are shown in Fig. 4B. It can be seen that each metal ion provides an almost distinct visual color with a concentration-dependent intensity. It should be noted that this proposed method is not limited to the numbers of analyte and any analyte that has a specific effect on the fluorescence of the three CDs can be distinguished. Theoretically, this method allows 256 × 256 × 256 discrete combinations of red, green, and blue values, giving millions of different combined colors that are not necessarily distinguishable.

It is well known that sensor arrays usually have problems in discriminating complicated samples. Therefore, the discrimination ability of the designed sensor array was also investigated to discriminate binary and ternary mixed samples of metal ions. The possible combinations of the three Hg2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+‏ ions and the three Au3+, Ag+, and Cu2+ ions at different concentrations were exposed to the sensor array (Figs. S5 and S6). The fluorescence responses obtained for the binary and ternary mixture samples were different from those for the single metal ions and still provide unique patterns to discriminate these samples. The PCA plots of the provided data sets are shown in Fig. 5A and B. Indeed, all binary and ternary mixtures of metal ions provided curves that were clearly different from those of the corresponding single metal ions. While some overlap can be seen at low concentrations, especially for the mixed samples of Hg2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+‏ ions, the PCA points generally extended in different directions with increasing the concentration.

Similar results were obtained for the visual fingerprint-like colors of the mixed samples, Fig. 5C and D, and these mixed samples again provided distinctly different colors with concentration–dependent intensities. However, since the quenching effect of Pb2+ was almost similar for the three CDs, the colors provided for the mixed samples containing Pb2+ were close to those obtained for single Pb2+, but still distinguishable. However, from a general point of view, based on these results, it can be expected that the proposed sensor array has the potential to discriminate complex samples.

The limit of detection (LOD) of the metal ions determination by the three CDs was calculated using the calibration curve of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs against the metal ions, all at the same concentration range of 0.02–1.0 µM, Table S1 and Fig. S7. While much lower LOD values could be obtained from the single-fluorescence-intensity-based measurements, since the sensing process of the proposed sensor array and RGB coding method is based on the visual color detection by the naked eye, for concentrations below 0.2 µM it became difficult to distinguish the resulting visual colors and subsequently to distinguish and determine the type and concentration of metal ions. Similarly, the PSA plots for metal ions below 0.2 µM showed significant miss classifications. Therefore, 0.2 µM should be considered as the practical LOD of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practicability of the sensor array in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To verify this potential in the identification and quantification of metal ions in unknown samples, the final concentrations of 2 and 8 µM of Hg2+, Cd2+, Au3+, and Cu2+ metal ions and their binary combinations were added to commercial mineral water samples as examples. A similar normalization and RGB coding were applied to the recorded fluorescence spectra to convert them into the visual colors. For the PCA analysis, the fluorescence data of these samples were augmented with the standard fluorescence data, and then the augmented matrix was analyzed by PCA. According to the fingerprint-like colors obtained for the unknown samples, Fig. 6, the metal ion was correctly identified in all samples. Moreover, the colors provided for each concentration of the unknown ions were exactly between the two colors produced by the higher and lower concentrations of that ion in the standard samples. Similar results were obtained from the PCA plots, where the PCA points of the unknown samples were all distributed on the corresponding curves of the standard samples and close to their actual concentration positions.

A verity of CDs can be found in the literature that have been used for the determination of different metal ions. it is well known that the surface groups of CDs are highly dependent on the initial material sources used for the synthesis of CDs, and also on the other parameters such as the synthesis method and the doped heteroatoms. The above results (Figs. 1 and S2) confirmed the doping heteroatoms in three types of CDs. Thus, the (N, S- doped CDs) b-CDs, (N, O- doped CDs) g-CDs, and (N, P- doped CDs) o-CDs contain C-O/C-S/C-N, CO/COOH/N-O/CN, and C-N/P-N/P-O/P = O groups, respectively. Moreover, according to the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, and N1s, the peak positions in the three CDs were slightly different from each other. Namely, o-CDs with the highest graphite N content [31], and g-CDs with the highest carboxyl content (C(O)-O) indicated different types and amounts of surface sites in b-, g-, and o-CDs [39].

Our experiment also showed that the fluorescence emission of CDs results from the degree of surface oxidation, surface groups, and graphite N content [24,31,54]. The interaction of the dopant atoms in CDs with metal ions can enhance their optical sensing performance. These heteroatoms can provide different surface functional groups and therefore, different binding affinities for different target ions, resulting in different distinct fluorescence responses for each metal ion. Therefore, the sensing properties of the CDs can be completely changed by different dopant atoms, which can affect not only the surface functional groups of the CDs, but also their electronic interaction with the metal ions.

The analytical performance of the proposed sensor array for the discrimination and determination of seven ions was compared with that of the previously reported fluorescence sensor arrays. So far, various fluorescent nanoparticles have been presented as sensor arrays. The significant interactions with metal ions, types of metal ions, linear range, and detection limit of some of them are summarized in Table 1. According to these results, the detection limits of the proposed sensor array were low or comparable to most of the previous reports. Moreover, in this report, three types of CDs with tunable emission colors were synthesized using PPD as the main source under different synthesis conditions. As far as we know, most of these reports, except ref [24], only used the para-isomer of phenylenediamine for the synthesis of long-emitting CDs [25,27,28,55]. The results indicated that the proposed method can be considered as one of the label-free, low-cost, sensitive, and environmentally friendly sensor arrays with high-throughput analysis capability.

In summary, we have reported three blue-, green-, and orange-emitting CDs using PPD as the carbon source under different synthesis conditions. The prepared CDs were used to construct a sensor array system that clearly discriminates seven metal ions of different concentrations. The RGB coding method proposed in the report provided a simple and visual way to identify of metal ions with distinct concentration-dependent colors for quantification purposes. The different fingerprint-like colors were successfully obtained for the unknown concentrations of the singular and binary mixed metal ions in mineral water samples. While determination of metal ions by the individual CDs through single-fluorescence-intensity-based measurement method showed lower LOD values (0.01 – 0.12 µM), for the proposed RGB coding method it was difficult to distinguish metal ions with concentrations below 0.2 µM. Thus, 0.2 µM was considered as the practical and naked eye LOD of the proposed method. This method is not limited to fluorescence data and can even be applied to sensor arrays with more than three channels.

Fluorescence quantum yield measurement method, XRD, and FTIR spectra of CDs, two-dimensional PCA score plots, and fluorescence responses of the sensor array to binary and ternary mixed samples of metal ions are presented in the Supporting Information file as Figure S1-S6.

Karami Sara: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shamsipur Mojtaba: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Barati Ali: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.111953.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent trends in macro-, micro-, and nanomaterial-based tools and strategies for heavy-metal detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Aragay, J. Pons, A. Merkoçi, Recent Trends in Macro-, Micro-, and Nanomaterial-Based Tools and Strategies for Heavy-Metal Detection, Chem. Rev. 111 (2011) 3433–3458.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanostructured sensors for detection of heavy metals: a review
